problem_summary: 2021 MCM/ICM Problem D ("How does music evolve?"): given four datasets (influence_data of influencer→follower pairs, full_music_data / data_by_artist with 13-14 audio characteristics, data_by_year time series), the team built objective, quantitative measures to (1) measure pairwise musical influence between artists and build a directed influence network, (2) quantify pairwise musical similarity from high-dimensional audio features and test whether within-genre vs between-genre similarity differs significantly, (3) identify which characteristics distinguish genres ("influential") and which are shared across artists ("contagious"), (4) detect "revolutionary" artists, and (5) characterize the dynamic evolution of music over ~100 years. Framed by the team as "How to analyze music objectively?"
models_used: Weighted directed influence network G(V,E,W): edge influencer->follower, edge weight I_ij = k_ij_tilde * m_ij_tilde * O_i, combining a decade-pairing indicator (k), a genre-pairing indicator (m, both row-normalized over a follower's influencers), and the influencer out-degree O_i

Modified Randomly Distributed Embedding (MRDE) for similarity: adapt Ma et al. 2018 PNAS RDE; randomly sample q = C(13,3) 3-feature embeddings, compute rough similarity sr_k = 1 - ||C_i - C_j||_2 per embedding, then similarity S_ij = E(Sr), the expectation over the embedding distribution (min-max normalized features)
Gaussian fitting of within-genre vs between-genre (and influence-connected vs not-connected) similarity distributions, plus Bayes error rate to quantify distributional separability/overlap
Central-node-per-genre construction: node percentage P_j = (sum_k I_jk*S_jk)/O_j weighting, then genre characteristic vector Ch_i as P_j-weighted average of member feature vectors (19x13 matrix M1)
Kruskal-Wallis non-parametric test with leave-one-feature-out p-value comparison to rank 'influential' (genre-distinguishing) and 'contagious' (artist-shared) characteristics
Closeness centrality on the directed graph: synthesized centrality C(i) = CC_out(i) - CC_in(i) to rank 'revolutionary' artists (high out-, low in-closeness)

Discrete/Fast Fourier Transform of per-characteristic yearly time series with a custom low-frequency-energy ratio indicator sigma = 2*sum_{m=1..4}|X[m]|^2 / sum|X[m]|^2, plus IFFT reconstruction from low-frequency components
key_techniques: Decomposing a single edge weight into three independently-justified, normalized indicators (temporal, genre, prominence) rather than a single ad-hoc score
Row-normalizing the temporal (k) and genre (m) indicators over the set of a follower's influencers so weights are comparable and interpretable as relative contributions

Using an ensemble of low-dimensional random projections (3 of 13 features) and taking the expectation to preserve high-dimensional interaction information instead of PCA, explicitly motivated against PCA losing interactions
Bayes error rate as a single scalar separability metric between two fitted Gaussian similarity distributions — turning 'are these distributions different?' into a number
Cross-validating one model with another: using the similarity network (G1) to validate the influence network (G) by showing influence-connected pairs have higher similarity (Bayes error 0.645)

Leave-one-out feature ablation measuring the change in Kruskal-Wallis p-value to attribute genre-discrimination power to individual features; iterative greedy elimination to rank-order 'contagious' features

Frequency-domain trend analysis: reducing a noisy 60-point yearly series to a low-frequency proportion scalar sigma, with IFFT reconstruction as visual evidence the low-frequency part captures the trend
Sanity-checking quantitative rankings against ground-truth domain knowledge (Bob Dylan Nobel, Chuck Berry rock pioneer, The Beatles) to argue face validity
why_it_won: "It converted a vague, subjective prompt ('how does music evolve / influence work') into a coherent suite of five rigorously-defined, mutually-reinforcing quantitative models, each tied to a specific sub-question and a specific dataset. Three things judges reward stand out: (1) a non-obvious, well-cited methodological choice — adapting the RDE framework from a 2018 PNAS paper for dimensionality handling, with an explicit argument for why it beats PCA (preserves feature interactions); (2) internal validation, where one model checks another (the similarity network validates the influence-edge definition via Bayes error rate, and a separate 'Test the Model' section validates the central-node construction by showing similarity drops to N(0.45,0.04) from N(0.85,0.06)); and (3) face-validity checks against real music history that make the abstract centrality rankings credible. The work is statistically literate (Kruskal-Wallis chosen correctly as non-parametric, Bayes error, Gaussian fitting) and signal-processing literate (DFT/FFT with a custom interpretable sigma indicator), signaling breadth. Each metric is a defined, defensible scalar rather than hand-waving."
section_highlights: "Structure maps one-to-one onto the restated sub-problems: Section 4.1 influence, 4.2 similarity (4.2.1 MRDE, 4.2.2 distributions/Bayes, 4.2.3 cross-validation), 4.3 roles of characteristics (4.3.1 influential / 4.3.2 contagious), 4.4 revolutionary artists, 4.5 dynamic evolution — so a judge can find any answer instantly. Strong use of numbered Definitions (4.1-4.3) and numbered equations gives a formal, paper-like rigor. Worked 'Example 1' (Avant-Garde->Classical, m_ij = 11/37 ≈ 0.2973) makes an abstract formula concrete. A dedicated Section 5 'Test the Model' is a distinct, named validation step rather than a buried sentence. Each result is stated with its fitted distribution and Bayes error number (e.g. N(0.806,0.078) vs N(0.758,0.095), Bayes error 0.770). Strengths/Weaknesses honestly names the aggregation imprecision and suggests LSTM/Pearson-correlation future work, and a separate one-page ICM-Society summary letter is included."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy figure/table corruption in this markdown rendering (OCR'd heatmaps, network graph, bubble chart show placeholder/garbled numbers), but more substantively the paper's own numbers are internally inconsistent and likely mislabeled: the text concludes 'similarity within genres is generally larger than between genres' yet the fitted means show within N(0.806) > between N(0.758) is consistent, while the Summary and prose elsewhere state the opposite ('similarity between genres is generally larger than within genres') — a real contradiction left unreconciled

Bayes error interpretation is muddled: a high Bayes error (0.77, 0.916, 0.645) means distributions OVERLAP heavily / are HARD to separate, yet the paper repeatedly reads large Bayes error as evidence the distributions are 'more different'; this is backwards and would worry a careful judge

MRDE 'rough similarity' sr = 1 - ||C_i - C_j|| can go negative (Euclidean distance in [0,sqrt(3)] after the 3-d embedding), so S_ij is not cleanly in [0,1] as claimed without truncation; the team acknowledges aggregation is 'not accurate enough'

Influence weight I_ij = k*m*O_i multiplies three indicators with no justification for the multiplicative form or for treating out-degree O_i (unbounded) on the same footing as two normalized [0,1] indicators — scale dominance by O_i is unaddressed

The decade indicator k_ij and genre indicator m_ij are identical for ALL artist pairs sharing the same (decade-pair) or (genre-pair), so the 'pairwise' influence is actually coarse-grained to decade/genre buckets, not truly artist-specific
sigma uses only the first 4 frequency bins as 'low-frequency' with no principled cutoff selection; threshold is arbitrary
Several typos/unfinished references in the text ('appendices???', 'Fig. ??', M1 vs M2 reuse of equation label) show rushed final editing
reusable_patterns: Restate the problem as an explicit bulleted list of sub-problems, then make every model section answer exactly one bullet — gives the paper a navigable, judge-friendly skeleton

Build an edge weight from several independently-motivated, separately-normalized indicators and combine them, instead of a single opaque score; document each indicator with a numbered Definition and a worked numerical example

When facing high-dimensional features, consider a random-subspace ensemble (sample many low-dim projections, aggregate by expectation) as a cited alternative to PCA, and explicitly argue why (preserves interactions)

Reduce 'are two groups different?' to a single scalar (Bayes error rate between fitted Gaussians, or a Kruskal-Wallis p-value) so results are comparable and tabulatable — but get the direction of interpretation right
Rank feature importance by leave-one-out ablation of a test statistic (delta p-value), and rank-order via greedy iterative elimination

Validate one model with another model and with external ground truth: use a second network to check the first, and check that your top-ranked entities match known real-world exemplars (named-artist sanity check)
Include a dedicated, named 'Test the Model' section that re-derives a quantity under a perturbation and shows it moves as predicted (central node => similarity drops)
Use frequency-domain analysis (FFT) plus a custom normalized energy-ratio indicator to summarize/denoise short time series, with IFFT reconstruction as the visual proof

Pick the statistically correct tool and cite it: Kruskal-Wallis for non-normal group comparisons, Bayesian decision theory for classification-error framing — citing canonical sources signals competence
Close with honest weaknesses and concrete, named future extensions (LSTM for forecasting, Pearson correlation for external drivers) rather than generic filler
